preamble: The transformation of an alveolar nasal /n/ into a voiced postalveolar fricative /ʒ/ is a documented phonetic shift, primarily occurring through a process known as palatalization. This sound change involves the articulation of the consonant moving towards the hard palate, often triggered by a neighboring palatal sound or a high front vowel. This shift results in a change in both the place of articulation (from alveolar to postalveolar) and the manner of articulation (from nasal to fricative).
phoneticEffects: Palatalization, Fricativization (change from nasal to fricative manner), Place Assimilation (from alveolar to postalveolar)

examples.note: General shift from alveolar nasal to voiced postalveolar fricative through palatalization, commonly represented as 'n to zh'.

sources: Kelton, H. (1887). Indian names of places near the Great Lakes. Detroit Free Press Printing Co.
Slavin, T. (2008). The Syntax and Semantics of Stem Composition in Ojicree. University of Toronto.
